passages.explanation: After the prayer, Jesus and his disciples cross the Kidron Valley to a garden. Judas knows this location because Jesus frequently met there with his disciples. Judas arrives with a detachment of Roman soldiers and officers from the chief priests and Pharisees, carrying lanterns, torches, and weapons. The force is substantial — this isn't a quiet arrest.

Jesus, knowing everything that's about to happen, steps forward and asks who they're looking for. They say Jesus of Nazareth. He answers 'I am he' — and when he says it, the entire group falls backward to the ground. The detail is striking: the people who came to arrest him can't even stand in his presence. He asks again, gets the same answer, and tells them if they're looking for him, they should let the others go. John notes this fulfils Jesus' own words about not losing any of those given to him. Jesus is managing his own arrest.

Peter draws a sword and cuts off the right ear of the high priest's servant, Malchus. Jesus tells Peter to put the sword away. His reasoning isn't tactical — it's theological: the Father has given him this cup to drink. He's not going to resist what he came to do.

The soldiers and officers seize and bind Jesus. They take him first to Annas, the father-in-law of Caiaphas, the current high priest. John reminds the reader that Caiaphas is the one who said it was better for one man to die for the people — connecting the political calculation from chapter 11 to the events now unfolding.

Peter and another disciple follow Jesus. The other disciple is known to the high priest and gets into the courtyard. Peter is left outside until the other disciple speaks to the servant girl at the door and gets Peter in. The girl asks Peter if he's one of Jesus' disciples. Peter says no — his first denial. He stands by a charcoal fire warming himself with the servants and officers.

The high priest questions Jesus about his disciples and his teaching. Jesus responds that he's always taught openly in synagogues and the temple — nothing was done in secret. He tells them to ask the people who heard him. An officer slaps Jesus for what he considers a disrespectful answer. Jesus responds calmly: if he said something wrong, point it out; if not, why hit him? Annas sends him bound to Caiaphas.

Peter is still warming himself by the fire. People ask if he's one of Jesus' disciples. He denies it — his second denial. Then a relative of Malchus, the man whose ear Peter cut off, says he saw Peter in the garden. Peter denies it again. Immediately the rooster crows — exactly as Jesus predicted in chapter 13.

Jesus is taken from Caiaphas to the Roman governor's headquarters, the Praetorium. It's early morning. The Jewish leaders won't enter the building because it would make them ritually unclean for Passover. Pilate comes out to them and asks what charges they're bringing. They give a vague answer: he's an evildoer. Pilate tells them to judge him by their own law. They say they aren't permitted to execute anyone — which John notes fulfils Jesus' prediction about the manner of his death. Crucifixion was a Roman execution method, not a Jewish one.

Pilate goes back inside and asks Jesus directly: are you the King of the Jews? Jesus asks whether Pilate is asking on his own or because others said it. Pilate is dismissive — he's not Jewish, and Jesus' own people handed him over. What did he do? Jesus says his kingdom is not of this world — if it were, his followers would have fought to prevent his arrest. Pilate presses: so you are a king? Jesus says that's Pilate's word, but he was born to testify to the truth, and everyone who belongs to the truth listens to him. Pilate responds with 'What is truth?' — and without waiting for an answer, goes back outside and tells the crowd he finds no basis for charges.

Pilate offers a custom: releasing one prisoner at Passover. He suggests releasing Jesus, 'the King of the Jews.' The crowd shouts for Barabbas instead — a robber. They choose a criminal over the man Pilate just declared innocent.
